questions.question: What two axes is a generative image model judged on, and by which metrics?
options: Speed and model size

Sample quality (FID: distribution distance in Inception feature space, lower better) and conditioning adherence (CLIP score: image-text embedding cosine, higher better) — plus human preference aggregated to Elo as ground truth
Loss value and gradient norm
Resolution and color depth
questions.explanation: Neither axis has a closed-form measure, so all three surviving metrics are proxies — and each has failure modes a determined researcher can game.

questions.question: How is FID computed?
options: Pixel-wise MSE between image sets

Extract Inception-v3 features (2048-D) for N real and N generated images, fit a Gaussian to each pool, then FID = ||μ_r − μ_g||² + Tr(Σ_r + Σ_g − 2(Σ_rΣ_g)^0.5) — the Fréchet distance between the two Gaussians
A CNN classifies real vs fake
Counting duplicate images
questions.explanation: Heusel et al. 2017. It measures distribution similarity, not individual image quality — which is both its strength and the root of its gaming surface.

questions.question: What are FID's known failure modes?
options: It has none — FID is reliable

Small-N bias (under-estimated covariance gives falsely LOW FID — use N ≥ 10,000), Inception-dependence (meaningless on domains far from ImageNet like faces or text images), and gameability by overfitting to the Inception prior
It only works on 299px images
It requires paired image sets
questions.explanation: CMMD (2024) addresses several: CLIP features instead of Inception, maximum-mean discrepancy instead of Fréchet — better at detecting subtle quality differences.

questions.question: How can CLIP score be gamed or misled?
options: It can't — cosine similarity is robust

CLIP has weak compositional reasoning ("a red cube on a blue sphere" often fails), short prompts mechanically score higher, and stuffing "high quality, 4k, masterpiece" into prompts inflates the score without improving image-text binding
Only by hacking the eval server
By using larger image resolutions
questions.explanation: A model can rank well on CLIP score without genuinely following complex prompts — the metric inherits every blind spot of the CLIP encoder it's built on.

questions.question: Why does human preference remain the ground truth despite its cost?
options: Humans are cheaper than GPUs

Head-to-head comparisons on shared prompts, aggregated to Elo/Bradley-Terry, capture the quality judgments automated proxies miss — benchmarks like PartiPrompts (1,600 prompts, 12 categories) standardize the prompt pool
Regulators mandate human evaluation
Preference scores never disagree with FID
questions.explanation: The full eval stack in practice: FID/CMMD for distribution health, CLIP score for adherence, preference arenas for the final ranking — knowing each metric's failure mode is what separates real improvements from gaming runs.
